claim: TLS 1.3 requires all public-key key exchange mechanisms to provide forward secrecy.

refs.span: all public-key based key exchange mechanisms now provide forward secrecy
refs.snapshot: Major differences from TLS 1.2 include the following. Static RSA and Diffie-Hellman cipher suites have been removed; all public-key based key exchange mechanisms now provide forward secrecy.

refs.span: every key exchange in TLS 1.3 provides forward secrecy
refs.snapshot: Unlike earlier versions, every key exchange in TLS 1.3 provides forward secrecy, so a compromised long-term key cannot decrypt past sessions.

note: straightforward true RFC claim, A+B distinct clusters

claim: HTTP/3 maps HTTP semantics over the QUIC transport protocol.

refs.span: HTTP/3 is a mapping of HTTP semantics over the QUIC transport protocol
refs.snapshot: This document defines HTTP/3. HTTP/3 is a mapping of HTTP semantics over the QUIC transport protocol, similar to but distinct from HTTP/2 over TCP.

refs.span: HTTP/3 runs over QUIC rather than TCP
refs.snapshot: HTTP/3 runs over QUIC rather than TCP, carrying HTTP semantics on top of QUIC streams.

claim: In RFC 2119 the keyword MUST denotes an absolute requirement of the specification.

refs.span: MUST This word, or the terms "REQUIRED" or "SHALL", mean that the definition is an absolute requirement of the specification
refs.snapshot: 1. MUST This word, or the terms "REQUIRED" or "SHALL", mean that the definition is an absolute requirement of the specification.

refs.span: MUST means the definition is an absolute requirement of the specification
refs.snapshot: Under BCP 14, MUST means the definition is an absolute requirement of the specification and cannot be ignored.

claim: An HTTP 200 status code indicates that the request has succeeded.

refs.span: The 200 (OK) status code indicates that the request has succeeded
refs.snapshot: 15.3.1. 200 OK. The 200 (OK) status code indicates that the request has succeeded.

refs.span: 200 OK indicates that the request has succeeded
refs.snapshot: The HTTP 200 OK indicates that the request has succeeded. The meaning of success depends on the request method.

claim: RFC 1918 reserves the 10.0.0.0/8 block for private internets.

refs.snapshot: The Internet Assigned Numbers Authority has reserved the following three blocks of the IP address space for private internets: 10.0.0.0 - 10.255.255.255 (10/8 prefix).

refs.span: 10.0.0.0/8 is allocated for private use
refs.snapshot: In the IANA special-purpose registry, 10.0.0.0/8 is allocated for private use per RFC 1918.

claim: The Pfizer-BioNTech vaccine BNT162b2 was 95% effective in preventing Covid-19 in its phase 3 trial.

refs.span: BNT162b2 was 95% effective in preventing Covid-19
refs.snapshot: A two-dose regimen of BNT162b2 conferred 95% protection against Covid-19 in persons 16 years of age or older. BNT162b2 was 95% effective in preventing Covid-19.

refs.span: vaccine efficacy was 95% in preventing Covid-19
refs.snapshot: Based on the trial, vaccine efficacy was 95% in preventing Covid-19 occurring at least 7 days after the second dose.

claim: The Moderna mRNA-1273 vaccine showed 94.1% efficacy at preventing Covid-19 illness.

refs.span: vaccine efficacy of 94.1% in preventing Covid-19 illness
refs.snapshot: The mRNA-1273 vaccine showed 94.1% efficacy at preventing Covid-19 illness, including severe disease. The trial reported a vaccine efficacy of 94.1% in preventing Covid-19 illness.

refs.span: 94.1% efficacy in preventing symptomatic Covid-19
refs.snapshot: CDC summarized the trial as demonstrating 94.1% efficacy in preventing symptomatic Covid-19.

claim: NVIDIA reported revenue of $130.5 billion for fiscal year 2025.

refs.span: revenue for fiscal 2025 of $130.5 billion, up 114% from a year ago
refs.snapshot: NVIDIA today reported revenue for fiscal 2025 of $130.5 billion, up 114% from a year ago.

refs.span: Revenue for fiscal year 2025 was $130.5 billion
refs.snapshot: Revenue for fiscal year 2025 was $130.5 billion, an increase of 114 percent from fiscal year 2024.

claim: QUIC version 1 is defined in RFC 9000.

refs.span: This document defines version 1 of the QUIC transport protocol
refs.snapshot: This document defines version 1 of the QUIC transport protocol. QUIC version 1 is identified by the value 0x00000001.

refs.span: QUIC version 1 (0x00000001) is specified by RFC 9000
refs.snapshot: In the QUIC versions registry, QUIC version 1 (0x00000001) is specified by RFC 9000.

note: identifier-number regression: 'version 1'/'RFC 9000' must NOT count as quantities; A+B

claim: TLS 1.3 completes a full handshake in one round trip (1-RTT).

refs.span: the full TLS 1.3 handshake requires one round trip
refs.snapshot: In the common case the full TLS 1.3 handshake requires one round trip before application data can be sent, a reduction from TLS 1.2.

refs.span: TLS 1.3 reduces the handshake to a single round trip
refs.snapshot: TLS 1.3 reduces the handshake to a single round trip, and 0-RTT can eliminate it for resumed sessions.

note: numeric '1.3' decimal kept and present in span; '1-RTT'/'one' dropped as single-digit/identifier; A+B

claim: RFC 8259 requires JSON text exchanged between systems to be encoded in UTF-8.

refs.span: JSON text exchanged between systems that are not part of a closed ecosystem MUST be encoded using UTF-8
refs.snapshot: 8.1. Character Encoding. JSON text exchanged between systems that are not part of a closed ecosystem MUST be encoded using UTF-8.

refs.span: JSON text must be encoded using UTF-8 when exchanged between systems
refs.snapshot: Per the specification, JSON text must be encoded using UTF-8 when exchanged between systems.

claim: RFC 6750 defines how OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens are sent in the Authorization request header field.

refs.span: the bearer token is transmitted in the Authorization request header field
refs.snapshot: When using the Authorization Request Header Field, the bearer token is transmitted in the Authorization request header field defined by HTTP/1.1.

refs.span: bearer tokens are sent in the Authorization request header field
refs.snapshot: Following RFC 6750, bearer tokens are sent in the Authorization request header field using the Bearer scheme.

claim: RFC 9114 prohibits using the HTTP Upgrade mechanism in HTTP/3.

refs.span: the Upgrade header field ... is not applicable to HTTP/3
refs.snapshot: HTTP/3 does not use the mechanism of HTTP/1.1; the Upgrade header field ... is not applicable to HTTP/3 and MUST NOT be sent.

claim: HTTP/3 uses QUIC as its transport.

refs.span: QUIC is the transport that HTTP/3 is built upon
refs.snapshot: The QUIC protocol serves as the transport that HTTP slash three is built upon in this revision.

refs.span: HTTP/3 relies on QUIC for transport
refs.snapshot: Architecturally, the third major HTTP version relies on the QUIC datagram layer for transport services.

refs.span: static RSA cipher suites are no longer supported
